docs.title: UiPath Agentic Practice Workshop
docs.text:
Learner's Handbook — a practical guide for hands-on exercises

Training Environment

  • Platform: cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs
  • Tenant: AgenticPractice
  • Contact: sergey.shumilov@uipath.com

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text: This workshop guides you through building real agentic automation solutions on the UiPath Platform. You'll work through two main use cases, each progressively adding intelligence and orchestration complexity.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Focused, domain-specific AI solutions consistently outperform general-purpose chatbots.

The exercises apply software engineering discipline — structured process design, systematic testing, and incremental iteration — to AI automation projects.

docs.title: 1. Categorizing Incidents
docs.text:
Build an intelligent ServiceNow incident triage agent, progressing through five phases:

Phase Focus Basic agent Foundational categorization using system prompts LLM with Context Ground the agent in real incident data to eliminate hallucinations Evaluations Test performance across standardized test cases Live Integration Connect to ServiceNow API — read and write real incidents Tools & Escalations Route complex cases to humans via Action Center

Start Exercise →

docs.title: 2. Invoice Matching Automation
docs.text:
Build a multi-component process combining AI agents, robots, human validation, and API integrations using Maestro orchestration.

Start Exercise →

docs.title: 3. Invoice Matching with IXP
docs.text:
Extend the invoice matching workflow with Intelligent eXtraction & Processing for automated data extraction from PDF invoices with confidence-based escalation.

Start Exercise →

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
Agent Builder — Low-code environment for creating, designing, and deploying agents.

Maestro — UiPath's orchestration platform for coordinating humans, robots, and AI agents across long-running enterprise processes.

IXP (Intelligent eXtraction & Processing) — Document processing technology with confidence scoring for automated data extraction from unstructured documents.

Action Center — UiPath interface for human escalation workflows, exception handling, and manual review.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: You've built a complete agentic orchestration. Here's what to do next.

docs.title: What You Accomplished
docs.text:
By completing these exercises, you built a production-pattern automation that combines:

Component Role Robots Structured, deterministic task execution AI Agents Intelligent decision-making and data processing Humans Judgment, validation, and exception handling APIs System integration and data exchange

docs.title: 1. Publish and Run Your Process
docs.text:
Publish your completed process to your personal workspace, then run it as you would any other UiPath process.

  • Open Maestro in your workspace
  • Publish the process
  • Trigger a test run with sample data
  • Review the execution trace

docs.title: 2. Explore Maestro Analytics
docs.text:
After a few runs, Maestro analytics become available:

  • Transaction-level execution details
  • End-to-end process flow visualization
  • Bottleneck identification
  • Exception and escalation rates

Open Maestro → Analytics to explore your process performance.

docs.title: Keep Iterating
docs.text:
The real learning happens in experimentation:

Prompt engineering - Modify your agent's system prompt - Test how changes affect categorization or matching decisions - Try different approaches for handling edge cases

Test the boundaries - Feed your agent unusual or ambiguous inputs - See where it fails or produces unexpected results - Design for those failure modes

Expand the process - Add a new document type to the matching process - Introduce a new escalation path - Connect to an additional system

docs.title: Learn More
docs.text:
Resource Description UiPath Academy Official learning paths for Agent Builder and Maestro UiPath Documentation API references, platform guides, release notes UiPath Community Forum for questions, shared automations, and tips

Questions?

Contact: sergey.shumilov@uipath.com

docs.title: Categorizing Incidents
docs.text: Build an intelligent ServiceNow incident triage agent

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: In this exercise you'll use UiPath Agent Builder to create an agent that automatically categorizes and routes ServiceNow incidents. You'll progress through five phases, each adding a new capability layer.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Basic Agent
docs.text:
Create a foundational categorization agent using system prompts. The agent analyzes incident descriptions and assigns categories without any external data sources.

You'll learn: - Agent Builder basics - Writing effective system prompts for categorization - Testing agent responses

docs.title: Phase 2 — LLM with Context
docs.text:
Ground your agent in real incident data to eliminate hallucinations. Instead of relying solely on the LLM's general knowledge, you'll provide structured context from your incident database.

You'll learn: - Data-anchoring techniques - Preventing AI-generated assumptions - Feeding structured data into prompts

docs.title: Phase 3 — Evaluations
docs.text:
Deploy an evaluation framework to systematically test your agent's performance across a standardized collection of test cases.

You'll learn: - Building test case collections - Running batch evaluations - Measuring and improving categorization accuracy

docs.title: Phase 4 — Live System Integration
docs.text:
Connect the agent to ServiceNow's live API — retrieve incident details from real systems and post categorization decisions back.

You'll learn: - API integration in Agent Builder - Reading from and writing to ServiceNow - Working with real operational data

docs.title: Phase 5 — Tools and Escalations
docs.text:
Implement escalation workflows via Action Center. Route complex or ambiguous incidents to human reviewers when automated handling isn't appropriate.

You'll learn: - Configuring Action Center escalations - Designing human-in-the-loop decision points - When (and how) to hand off to humans

docs.title: Platform
docs.text:
All exercises run in UiPath Agent Builder, a low-code environment that lets you create, design, and deploy agents without deep coding experience.

Training Environment

Log in at cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs using tenant AgenticPractice.

docs.title: LLM with Context
docs.text: Ground your agent in real data to eliminate hallucinations

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text: In this phase you extend the basic categorization agent by anchoring its responses to actual incident information. Without grounding, the LLM may generate plausible-sounding but incorrect category assignments. With context, the agent's decisions are traceable to real data.

docs.title: Key Concept: Data Anchoring
docs.text:
Data anchoring means providing the LLM with structured, factual context before asking it to make a decision. Instead of:

"Here is an incident description. Categorize it."

You send:

"Here is an incident description. Here is the relevant data from our incident history and category definitions. Based only on this data, categorize the incident."

This constrains the model's output space to what is factually supported.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
After completing this phase, your agent will:

  • Pull incident context before generating a response
  • Base categorization decisions on actual data rather than inference
  • Produce more consistent, auditable results

← Back to Overview | Next: Tools and Escalations →

docs.title: Tools and Escalations
docs.text: Route complex cases to humans via Action Center

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text: In this final phase you implement human escalation workflows. Not every incident can — or should — be handled automatically. This phase teaches you to recognize those cases and route them to the right people.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text: Automation works best when it knows its own limits. An agent that escalates correctly is more valuable than one that confidently handles everything — including cases it shouldn't.

docs.title: Escalation Triggers
docs.text:
Configure your agent to escalate when:

  • Categorization confidence is below a defined threshold
  • The incident involves multiple potential categories with similar scores
  • The incident description is ambiguous, incomplete, or contradictory
  • Specific priority levels or impact categories require human review

docs.title: Action Center Integration
docs.text:
Action Center is the UiPath interface for human escalation. When your agent triggers an escalation:

  1. A task is created in Action Center with the incident details
  2. A human reviewer receives the task
  3. The reviewer categorizes and resolves the task
  4. The workflow continues with the human-provided decision

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
After completing this phase, your agent will:

  • Automatically handle clear-cut incidents
  • Escalate ambiguous or high-risk cases to humans
  • Resume the workflow once human input is received

← Back to Overview

docs.title: Invoice Matching Automation
docs.text: 2-Way matching with standard orchestration

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: In this exercise you'll build a comprehensive multi-component automation using Maestro to orchestrate AI agents, robots, human validators, and external APIs into a single end-to-end invoice matching process.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text:
Agentic orchestration, implemented in Maestro, empowers enterprise customers to coordinate their long-running enterprise processes between humans, robots, and AI agents, working seamlessly across different systems of records and engagement.

Maestro lets you design, implement, monitor, operate, and optimize long-running processes at scale by combining three participant types:

Participant Role Robot Structured, rule-based task execution AI Agent Intelligent decision-making Human Judgment, validation, exceptions

docs.title: Five-Step Implementation
docs.text:
Follow these steps in order to build the complete process:

  1. Create BPMN Diagram — Design the end-to-end process flow
  2. Add a Robot — Automate structured task execution
  3. Add an Agent — Add AI-driven decision-making
  4. Add a Human — Configure validation and escalation checkpoints
  5. Add API Calls — Connect to external systems to complete the workflow

Training Environment

Log in at cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs using tenant AgenticPractice.

docs.title: Step 3 — Add an Agent
docs.text: Add AI-driven decision-making to your process

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Integrate an AI agent into the Maestro process. The agent handles the parts of the workflow that require understanding, reasoning, and judgment — things a robot alone can't do.

docs.title: What the Agent Does
docs.text:
The invoice matching agent:

  1. Receives invoice documents from the robot
  2. Extracts key invoice data (vendor, amount, line items, PO references)
  3. Queries the ERP system for the corresponding Purchase Order
  4. Compares invoice data against the PO
  5. Makes a matching decision: approve, reject, or escalate

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

← Step 2: Add a Robot | Next: Add a Human →

docs.title: Step 5 — Add API Calls
docs.text: Connect to external systems to complete the workflow

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Complete the end-to-end process by adding API integrations that connect your Maestro process to external systems of record.

docs.title: What API Calls Enable
docs.text:
With API integration, your process can:

  • Read Purchase Order data from the ERP system
  • Post matching decisions back to the invoicing system
  • Trigger downstream actions (payment approval, GL posting, notifications)
  • Update status fields in connected systems

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

docs.title: What You've Built
docs.text:
After completing all five steps, you have a fully functional invoice matching process that:

  • Uses a robot to retrieve invoice documents
  • Uses an agent to extract data and make matching decisions
  • Routes exceptions to humans via Action Center
  • Integrates with external systems via API to read PO data and post results

← Step 4: Add a Human | Back to Overview

docs.title: Step 4 — Add a Human
docs.text: Configure validation and escalation checkpoints

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add human intervention points to handle cases the agent flags as exceptions — invoices where automatic matching confidence is low, amounts exceed approval thresholds, or discrepancies require review.

docs.title: When Humans Are Needed
docs.text:
Automate the routine; escalate the exceptions. Humans are brought in when:

  • Invoice and PO data don't match cleanly
  • The agent's confidence in a decision is below threshold
  • Invoice amounts exceed an automatic approval limit
  • The invoice references a PO that can't be found

docs.title: Action Center Integration
docs.text:
Human tasks appear in Action Center, where reviewers can:

  • See the invoice and matched PO data side-by-side
  • Approve or reject the match
  • Add notes explaining the decision
  • Route to additional approvers if needed

The workflow resumes automatically once the human task is completed.

docs.title: Step 2 — Add a Robot
docs.text: Automate structured task execution

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add a robotic process automation component to handle the structured, rule-based parts of the workflow — tasks that are deterministic and don't require intelligent judgment.

docs.title: What the Robot Does
docs.text:
In the invoice matching process, the robot handles document retrieval and preparation:

  • Connects to the source system
  • Fetches invoice documents
  • Prepares them for processing by subsequent workflow participants

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

← Step 1: Create BPMN Diagram | Next: Add an Agent →

docs.title: Step 1 — Create BPMN Diagram
docs.text: Design your end-to-end process flow

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Before building anything in Maestro, model the process visually using BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation). The diagram becomes the blueprint for everything you'll implement in subsequent steps.

docs.title: What is BPMN?
docs.text:
BPMN is a standardized visual language for documenting business processes. It lets you represent:

  • Sequence of activities (who does what, and when)
  • Decision points (gateways)
  • Participants (swimlanes for humans, robots, agents, systems)
  • Events (start, end, exceptions)

docs.title: Your Process Diagram
docs.text:
The invoice matching process involves these participants:

  • Robot — retrieves invoices and documents
  • AI Agent — validates and matches invoice data against purchase orders
  • Human — reviews exceptions and approves edge cases
  • External System — ERP / PO database queried via API

docs.title: Invoice Matching with IXP
docs.text: 2-Way matching with Intelligent eXtraction & Processing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This exercise builds on the standard invoice matching process by adding IXP (Intelligent eXtraction & Processing) for automated data extraction from PDF invoices. IXP handles unstructured documents and provides confidence scores that drive automated escalation decisions.

docs.title: What IXP Adds
docs.text: Capability Description PDF extraction Reads and extracts data from unstructured invoice PDFs Confidence scoring Each extracted field gets a confidence score Automated escalation Low-confidence results automatically route to human review

docs.title: How It Fits the Process
docs.text:
The full workflow:

Robot retrieves PDFs
  → Agent extracts data using IXP
      → High confidence: Agent queries ERP for PO data
          → Agent validates and decides (match/reject)
      → Low confidence: Escalate to human for review
  → API calls complete the process

docs.title: Five-Step Implementation
docs.text:
1. Create BPMN Process — Design the IXP-enhanced workflow
  2. Configure a Robot — Set up invoice PDF retrieval
  3. Configure an Agent — Extract data, query ERP, validate, decide
  4. Configure Human Validation — Handle low-confidence escalations
  5. Configure API Integration — Connect to external systems

Training Environment

Log in at cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs using tenant AgenticPractice.

docs.title: Step 3 — Configure an Agent
docs.text: Extract, query, validate, and decide

docs.title: Goal
docs.text:
Configure the agent to perform three critical functions using IXP:

  1. Extract invoice data from PDFs using IXP with confidence-based escalation
  2. Query the ERP system for the corresponding Purchase Order data
  3. Validate and decide — match, reject, or escalate

docs.title: IXP Extraction
docs.text:
When the agent receives a PDF invoice, it uses IXP to extract structured data:

  • Vendor name and ID
  • Invoice number and date
  • Line items (description, quantity, unit price)
  • Total amount
  • Referenced PO number

Each extracted field includes a confidence score (0–100%). You'll configure a threshold — fields below the threshold trigger escalation.

docs.title: Confidence-Based Logic
docs.text:
For each extracted field:
  IF confidence >= threshold:
    → Proceed with extracted value
  ELSE:
    → Flag for human review

docs.title: ERP Query
docs.text: Once extraction is complete (or after human correction of low-confidence fields), the agent queries the ERP for the Purchase Order referenced on the invoice.

docs.title: Matching Decision
docs.text:
The agent compares: - Invoice vendor → PO vendor - Invoice line items → PO line items - Invoice total → PO total (within tolerance)

Result: Match, Reject, or Escalate for review

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

← Step 2: Configure a Robot | Next: Configure Human Validation →

docs.title: Step 5 — Configure API Integration
docs.text: Connect to external systems

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Complete the IXP-enhanced process by adding API integrations that connect Maestro to the external systems needed to retrieve PO data and post matching results.

docs.title: API Connections in This Process
docs.text: Integration Purpose ERP / PO system Read Purchase Order details for matching Invoice system Post matching decisions (approved/rejected) Notification system (Optional) Alert stakeholders of exceptions

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
Content migration in progress

Detailed steps and screenshots will be added here. In the meantime, refer to the original training site.

docs.title: What You've Built
docs.text:
After completing all five steps, your IXP-enhanced process:

  • Robot retrieves invoice PDFs from source
  • Agent + IXP extracts structured data with confidence scoring
  • Human validation corrects low-confidence extractions
  • Agent queries ERP, validates, and makes matching decisions
  • API calls post results and trigger downstream actions

← Step 4: Configure Human Validation | Back to Overview

docs.title: Step 4 — Configure Human Validation
docs.text: Handle low-confidence escalations

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Configure the human validation step that activates when IXP extraction confidence falls below your defined threshold.

docs.title: When Humans Are Needed
docs.text:
In the IXP workflow, human validation covers two scenarios:

  1. Low-confidence extraction — one or more fields were extracted with low confidence; a human verifies or corrects the extracted data before matching proceeds
  2. Matching exceptions — after extraction, the agent cannot make a clear match/reject decision

docs.title: The Human Task
docs.text:
In Action Center, the reviewer sees:

  • The original PDF invoice (for reference)
  • The IXP-extracted values with confidence scores highlighted
  • Fields flagged for review
  • Form fields to confirm or correct each value

Once submitted, the corrected data flows back into the process and the agent proceeds with matching.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:

← Step 3: Configure an Agent | Next: Configure API Integration →

docs.title: Step 2 — Configure a Robot
docs.text: Set up invoice PDF retrieval

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Configure the robotic component to retrieve invoice PDF documents from the source system and pass them to the agent for IXP extraction.

docs.title: What the Robot Does
docs.text:
1. Connects to the document source (shared drive, email, DMS)
  2. Identifies and retrieves unprocessed invoice PDFs
  3. Passes the documents to the Maestro process for the next step

← Step 1: Create BPMN Process | Next: Configure an Agent →

docs.title: Step 1 — Create BPMN Process
docs.text: Design the IXP-enhanced workflow

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Create the BPMN diagram for the IXP-based invoice matching process. This version differs from the standard process by including the IXP extraction step and the confidence-based branching logic.

docs.title: Key Differences from Standard Process
docs.text:
The IXP process adds:

  • An explicit extraction step after the robot delivers the invoice PDF
  • A confidence gate: high-confidence results flow to automated matching, low-confidence results route to human review
  • A human review task specifically for validating extracted data (not just matching decisions)
